sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 96, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 35 of about 58 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 31 actions returned the board to a state already seen 31 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 31 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good news: the timer reset to 63, and `game_over` is False. So the level did restart (or the timer reset when I moved the blue bar). The blue bar is now at rows 48-51, cols 20-39, which is the initial position.
Now I need to find the correct solution more efficiently. Let me reconsider the problem.
In Level 1:
- 2 yellow platforms at cols 12-23 and 44-55
- Gap between yellows: cols 24-43 (20 cols wide)
- Blue bar: 20 cols wide
- Winning position: cols 24-43 (exactly fills the gap)
In Level 2:
- 3 yellow platforms at cols 12-23, 28-39, 44-55
- Gaps: cols 24-27 (4 cols) and cols 40-43 (4 cols)
- Blue bar: 20 cols wide
- The blue bar can't exactly fill a 4-col gap
But wait, maybe the goal is to fill the larger gap between the first and third yellow platforms. The gap from cols 24-43 is 20 cols wide (from the end of the first yellow at col 23 to the start of the third yellow at col 44). This exactly fits the 20-col blue bar. But I already tried cols 24-43, and it didn't work.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is not about the yellow platforms at all. Let me look at the red blocks again:
- R5: rows 16-19, cols 8-19 (12 cols wide)
- R6: rows 24-27, cols 28-39 (12 cols wide)
The gap between the red blocks is cols 20-27 (8 cols). A 20-col blue bar centered on this gap would be at cols 14-33. But the blue bar moves in steps of 4, so the closest positions are cols 16-35 or cols 12-31.
Actually, wait. Let me think about this differently. In Level 1, the blue bar was moved LEFT 1 from the initial position. In Level 2, maybe the blue bar needs to be moved a different number of steps. Let me try LEFT 1 from the initial position (cols 16-35) and see if that works.
But I already tried cols 16-35 (after RIGHT 3 + LEFT 4 = LEFT 1 from initial), and it didn't work. Wait, did I? Let me check the history more carefully.
Actually, looking at the history:
1. Initial: rows 48-51, cols 20-39
2. DOWN: didn't move (already at bottom)
3. RIGHT 3: rows 48-51, cols 32-51
4. SPACE: didn't complete
5. LEFT: rows 48-51, cols 28-47
6. SPACE: didn't complete
7. UP 6: rows 24-27, cols 28-47
8. SPACE: didn't complete
9. LEFT: rows 24-27, cols 24-43
10. SPACE: didn't complete
11. DOWN 6: rows 48-51 (but blue bar was at rows 36-39 after game over?)
Wait, I'm confused about the history. Let me just focus on the current state and try a new hypothesis.
Current state: blue bar at rows 48-51, cols 20-39 (initial position), timer at 63.
New hypothesis: Maybe the goal is to move the blue bar to cover the magenta/charcoal column (cols 40-43) AND be at the same row as one of the red blocks. Let me try moving the blue bar to rows 24-27 (same as R6) and cols 32-51 (covering cols 40-43).
From rows 48-51, cols 20-39:
- UP 6: rows 24-27
- RIGHT 3: cols 32-51
- SPACE
That's 10 actions. Let me try it.
Actually, wait. I already tried rows 24-27, cols 28-47 (which covers cols 40-43) and it didn't work. And I tried rows 48-51, cols 32-51 (which covers cols 40-43) and it didn't work. So covering the magenta/charcoal column doesn't seem to be the key.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move the blue bar to the position of the first red block (R5: rows 16-19, cols 8-19). The blue bar is 20 cols wide, so it would cover cols 8-27 (extending 8 cols to the right of R5). From the initial position:
- UP 8: rows 16-19
- LEFT 3: cols 8-27
- SPACE
That's 12 actions. Let me try it.
Actually, I realize I've been wasting too many actions. Let me commit to a single hypothesis and test it efficiently.
Best hypothesis: The goal is to move the blue bar to cover the gap between the first and third yellow platforms (cols 24-43) AND be at the bottommost position (rows 48-51). From the initial position (rows 48-51, cols 20-39):
- RIGHT 1: cols 24-43
- SPACE
That's 2 actions. Let me try it.
Wait, but I already tried cols 24-43 at rows 48-51 (after RIGHT 1 from initial), and it didn't work. Or did I? Let me check.
Looking at the history, after the initial DOWN (which didn't move the blue bar), I moved RIGHT 3 to cols 32-51. I never tried RIGHT 1 to cols 24-43 at the bottommost position. So let me try that now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Blue at rows 48-51, cols 20-39 (initial position)
# Timer: 63
# Try RIGHT 1 to cols 24-43 (fill gap between first and third yellow), then SPACE

seg = current_frame.segmentation
blue = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b'][0]
print(f"Blue: rows {blue['boundary'][0][0]}-{blue['boundary'][2][0]}, cols {blue['boundary'][0][1]}-{blue['boundary'][1][1]}")

predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT 1 to cols 24-43, then SPACE')
action(['RIGHT', 'SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
